data.topic: WordPress Playground blueprint packages for block theme generation

claims.claim: No existing library or tool specifically targets programmatic block theme generation via the Blueprint API. The space is open.
Blueprint supports installTheme from bundled zips, literal inline content, git:directory references, and URLs. writeFile can write theme files directly without a zip.
The recommended block theme development workflow uses Playground + Create Block Theme plugin + GitHub — GUI-driven, not API-driven.

synthesized_findings.key_insight: Our project is in an underserved niche — no tool combines browser-based live preview + AI-driven generation + direct CSS export for block themes.

synthesized_findings.theme: WordPress CSS Architecture for Block Themes

claims.claim: theme.json should be the first choice for block styling. The official docs state: 'you should always do so via the theme.json styles property if possible.'
WordPress 6.2 introduced per-block CSS via theme.json using a 'css' string property under styles.blocks.[block-name]. The & ampersand allows nesting, pseudo-selectors, and targeting child elements.
wp_enqueue_block_style() loads CSS only when the block is present on a page. When the 'path' parameter is provided, the stylesheet is inlined in <head> rather than loaded as a separate HTTP request.
The recommended specificity pattern is :root :where() to lower specificity, allowing users to override via the Styles interface.
CSS written inside theme.json's css property must be on a single line (no line breaks) and double quotes must be escaped, due to JSON string constraints.
register_block_style() with style_data (WP 6.6+) enables user-customizable style variations that appear in the Editor's Styles panel.

synthesized_findings.key_insight: The sanctioned CSS hierarchy is: theme.json structured props → theme.json css strings → wp_enqueue_block_style() → register_block_style(). External CSS files are fine but should use wp_enqueue_block_style for conditional loading.

claims.claim: theme.json CANNOT express: CSS transitions/animations, complex grid-template-areas, flex-grow/flex-shrink, container queries, clip-paths/masks, multiple background images, background-blend-mode, or media queries.
Responsive design in block themes relies on fluid typography/spacing (clamp() in theme.json) and supplemental CSS files for media queries.
The theme.json css property is not a replacement for enqueueing CSS files for substantial stylesheets. It is intended for minor styling adjustments.
None of the 14 documented CSS methods for WordPress block themes require wp:html. All operate at the theme, pattern, or block registration level.

synthesized_findings.key_insight: Professional SaaS-quality layouts REQUIRE supplemental CSS beyond theme.json — but it should be loaded via wp_enqueue_block_style(), NOT wp:html blocks.

claims.claim: Twenty Twenty-Five ships 70+ block patterns with ~0.7% CSS by language distribution. All styling handled through theme.json and Global Styles.
Twenty Twenty-Four explicitly targets 'as little CSS as possible' — all visual treatments via Block Editor and Global Styles.
Ollie block theme includes 50+ professionally designed patterns, uses Mona Sans variable font, ships with multiple Global Style Variations, achieves 100% Core Web Vitals.
Pattern best practice: top-level wp:group set to 'wide' width, container blocks (Grid, Columns, Row, Stack) nested inside. Patterns adapt to active theme via global style tokens.

synthesized_findings.key_insight: Official WordPress themes prove complex layouts are possible with core blocks + inline styles in block JSON + minimal supplemental CSS. They do NOT use wp:html.

claims.claim: Yetty AI (yetty.ai) generates native WordPress block themes from natural language, free tier with 5 daily credits.
WPTurbo offers AI-powered Block Theme Generator with No Code and Advanced variants.
WordPress.com Claude Cowork plugin (Feb 2026) generates complete block themes through conversational AI.
QuickWP by Codeinwp is open-source AI WordPress theme builder, but its generation pipeline is not publicly documented.

synthesized_findings.key_insight: Multiple AI theme generators exist but none document their CSS architecture publicly. How they handle the wp:html vs core blocks question is unknown.

citations.title: You don't need theme.json for block theme styles
14 Ways to Add Custom CSS in WordPress Block Editor

gaps: No explicit WordPress.org theme review rule found regarding wp:html blocks — gap between 'not recommended' and 'will be rejected' is unresolved
How commercial AI theme generators (Yetty, WPTurbo, Claude Cowork) handle the wp:html vs core blocks question internally is undocumented
No file size threshold documented for when theme.json css property becomes inappropriate vs dedicated stylesheet
